name: Nobelium
name_origin: Named in honor of Alfred Nobel, the Swedish chemist and inventor of dynamite who established the Nobel Prizes.

discovery.discoverer: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (Dubna) team, with prior contested claims by Nobel Institute (Sweden) in 1957 and Lawrence Berkeley team in 1958

occurrence.abundance_summary: Synthetic element not found in nature; produced by bombarding curium or californium targets with carbon, oxygen, or neon ions in heavy-ion accelerators, only a few atoms at a time.

compounds.name: Nobelium(II) chloride
compounds.summary: Inferred dichloride observed only on the tracer scale; notable as evidence of nobelium's preference for the +2 oxidation state, which is unusual among the actinides.

uses: Scientific research into the chemistry of the heaviest actinides
Studies of relativistic effects on electronic structure and oxidation-state preferences
reactivity_summary: Bulk metallic nobelium has never been prepared. Tracer-scale chemistry shows that, uniquely among the actinides, nobelium is most stable in the +2 oxidation state because of the closed-shell 5f14 configuration of No(II); the +3 state is more difficult to reach than for neighboring actinides.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Nobelium-259 has the longest half-life (about 58 minutes). No-255 and No-254 are commonly produced in heavy-ion fusion experiments and have half-lives of minutes.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
IUPAC and IUPAP Joint Working Party reports on transfermium element discoveries
